title: Roast parsnips
subtitle: Caramelised edges, soft centres
excerpt: Parsnips par-boiled until nearly tender, then roasted in hot fat until the edges are dark and sweet. The natural sugars caramelise where they catch the heat.
sourceNotes: Standard British roast parsnip method. Par-boiling ensures the centres cook through before the edges burn.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Par-boil up to 4 hours ahead and leave to steam-dry. Roast when needed.

glossaryTerms.definition: Partially cooking vegetables in boiling salted water before roasting, so the centres are nearly tender before they go into the oven.
The browning of natural sugars under heat, which produces the sweet, dark, slightly bitter flavour on the edges of roasted root vegetables.

content.text: Parsnips have a high natural sugar content. This makes the edges catch and caramelise quickly in a hot oven while the centres take longer to cook through. Par-boiling solves this: the centres are soft before roasting begins, so the exterior can colour without the middle staying raw.

items.prepNote: peeled, halved lengthways, large ones quartered

content.text: Heat the oven to 200 degrees C fan.
the parsnips in well-salted boiling water for 5 minutes until just starting to soften at the edges. Drain and leave to steam-dry in the colander for 5 minutes.
Heat the duck fat or oil in the roasting tin in the oven for 5 minutes until shimmering. Add the parsnips and turn them in the hot fat. Add the thyme sprigs if using.
Roast for 30 to 35 minutes, turning once, until the edges are deeply
. If using honey, drizzle it over the parsnips for the last 10 minutes of cooking.

items.symptom: The parsnips are catching and burning on the edges before the centres are tender
items.fix: Always par-boil for a minimum of 5 minutes. The centre must be half-cooked before the parsnip goes into a hot oven.

items.symptom: The honey glaze is blackening and bitter
items.cause: Honey was added too early in the cooking.
items.fix: Add honey only in the last 10 minutes. Any earlier and the sugars will burn before the parsnips are done.

items.symptom: The parsnips are soft and pale, not caramelised
items.cause: The fat was not hot enough when the parsnips went in, or the oven temperature was too low.
items.fix: Heat the fat until shimmering before adding the parsnips. A cold or warm fat will steam rather than roast them.

items.symptom: The parsnips are sticking to the tin
items.cause: Turned too early before the caramelised edge has set.
items.fix: Leave for at least 20 minutes before the first turn. They release cleanly once a crust has formed.

content.text: Add a teaspoon of ground cumin or coriander to the fat before adding the parsnips for a spiced version. Maple syrup in place of honey gives a deeper, less floral sweetness. For the combined version with carrots, see the honey-roast carrots and parsnips recipe.

Roast parsnips are a British winter vegetable staple, on the Christmas table and Sunday roasts throughout the autumn and winter months. Parsnips were a significant food crop in Britain before the potato arrived from the Americas. Their relegation to a side vegetable came with the potato's rise in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries.
